plan/accessor-model.md.text: I dont like this, loosing this.cs in module, btw, it should be 'module' not 'modules'
plan/accessor-model.md.anchor: - The flat action registry `modules/this.cs` leaves `app.@this`'s public surface and becomes `module/registry.cs` (no `this.cs`, so no `module.@this` claim). It's held on an internal field; the 6 `app.Modules.*` call sites (`GetCodeGenerated`, `Discover`, `Describe`, `Contains`, `Remove`) reach it through that field.
plan/accessor-model.md.author: user

plan/accessor-model.md.text: Agreed ‚Äî reversed the demote. module/this.cs = module.@this stays at app.module (app.module["file"], app.module.list); the 6 registry ops stay methods on it. The .list-vs-list-action collision isn't real (member vs indexer key). No app.module.current since modules are dispatched, not navigated ‚Äî so it's a no-.current service, not an exception. Fixed the modules‚Üímodule wording too.
plan/accessor-model.md.anchor: - **No `app.module.current`** ‚Äî action modules are dispatched, not navigated; nothing is ever "the current module." That is the *only* way module differs from goal, and it's the same way type/channel/event/format differ. So module is not special ‚Äî it is a no-`.current` service.
plan/accessor-model.md.author: architect

plan/nullability.md.text:
lets change ctx to context, keep the same name everywhere.

while we are at it, are the other objects that nullable but shouldn¬¥t really be?? maybe check that.
plan/nullability.md.author: user

plan/nullability.md.text: Both done. ctx‚Üícontext everywhere folded into this pass (214 identifiers across 36 files). And I checked the others: 5 more structural back-refs are over-nullable and should flip too ‚Äî steps‚ÜíGoal, step‚ÜíGoal, channel‚ÜíActor, channel‚ÜíChannels, channels‚ÜíActor. 2 are init-only and need a lifecycle check before flipping (GoalCall.Action, IEvent.Step) ‚Äî a call/binding may legitimately exist unbound. Added a table. app.Parent stays nullable (root has no parent).
plan/nullability.md.author: architect

plan/type-entity.md.text: my feeling is that instead of returnin System.Type, we would be returning 'type', a plang version of System.Type, isn¬¥t that what Data.Type does?
plan/type-entity.md.anchor: The biggest and riskiest piece. It is here because the accessor model demands it: `app.type["int"]` is only worth the keystrokes if what comes back is *an object you work with*. Today it would be a raw `System.Type`, with the actual per-type knowledge scattered. This consolidates that knowledge into a real entity.
plan/type-entity.md.author: user

plan/type-entity.md.text: Exactly that ‚Äî and you named it better than I did. type.@this IS PLang's System.Type, and data.Type returns it. Today data.ClrType hands back a raw System.Type?; the promotion makes data.Type return the entity instead. Sharpened the opening to lead with that framing.
plan/type-entity.md.anchor: The biggest and riskiest piece. The frame (Ingi's, and it's the right one): **`type.@this` is PLang's version of `System.Type`** ‚Äî a real object that knows its own name, CLR type, scheme, and valid-values ‚Äî and `data.Type` returns *that*, not a raw `System.Type`. That's exactly what `data.Type` *should* do; today it doesn't (`data.ClrType` hands back a bare `System.Type?`), and the per-type knowledge is scattered across `System.Type` + `builder.Types.Entry` + scheme strings. This consolidates it into the entity. The accessor model demands it too: `app.type["int"]` is only worth the keystrokes if what comes back is an object you work with.
plan/type-entity.md.author: architect
